item.title: ShinyHunters PeopleSoft breaches, Kemp LoadMaster CVE-2026-8037, and LLM security tooling
U.S. Targets Russian Signal/WhatsApp Hackers as WhatsApp Adds Usernames, Mustang Panda Hits India
U.S. Bounties Russian Messaging Hackers as Mustang Panda and SimpleHelp Exploits Surface
Microsoft Purges StegoAd Edge Extensions as libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 PoC Emerges
Digest 2026-06-29 00:00 UTC
item.content:encoded:
Nissan discloses employee data breach linked to Oracle zero-day attacks — Must Read

Nissan disclosed a data breach affecting current and former employees after ShinyHunters exploited a zero-day in Oracle PeopleSoft (CVE-2026-35273) to steal sensitive personal and financial information.

  • Nissan is notifying current and former employees of a data breach after threat actors exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft vulnerability in a campaign linked to the ShinyHunters extortion group.
  • The exposed personal information includes Social Security numbers, banking details, contact information, and dependent data, affecting employees in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil.
  • Oracle confirmed the zero-day vulnerability as CVE-2026-35273 and released emergency mitigations after Mandiant reported exploitation between May 27 and June 9.
  • ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for breaching over 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, and has since started leaking stolen data from other victims.

Critical SimpleHelp flaw exploited to deploy new stealer malware — Must Read

Attackers are exploiting a critical authentication bypass in SimpleHelp (CVE-2026-48558) to deploy TaskWeaver and the new cross-platform Djinn Stealer, which steals cloud, developer, and AI coding assistant credentials from Windows, macOS, and Linux systems.

  • A threat actor exploited CVE-2026-48558, an authentication bypass in SimpleHelp, to create an unauthorized technician session on an internet-facing server.
  • The attacker deployed two previously undocumented pieces of malware: TaskWeaver, a JavaScript-based loader, and Djinn Stealer, a cross-platform information stealer that harvests credentials from Windows, macOS, and Linux.
  • Djinn Stealer targets AI development tools, cloud provider credentials, Git configurations, SSH keys, package managers, and cryptocurrency wallets.
  • The malware encrypts the collected data with AES-256-GCM using an RSA-2048 public key embedded in the loader before sending it to a command-and-control server.
  • Blackpoint warns that stealing local AI assistant configuration files could grant attackers the same downstream access to repositories, cloud accounts, and internal APIs that developers granted to their AI agents.

Public PoC Released for Critical libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 Client-Side SSH Flaw — Must Read

A public proof-of-concept exploit has been released for a critical memory corruption vulnerability (CVE-2026-55200) in the widely embedded libssh2 SSH client library, allowing malicious servers to potentially execute code on connecting clients; urgent patching or outbound SSH restriction is needed, especially given libssh2's hidden presence in many applications.

  • A public proof-of-concept has been released for CVE-2026-55200, a critical vulnerability in the libssh2 client-side SSH library that allows a malicious server to corrupt memory and possibly execute code on connecting clients without any user interaction.
  • libssh2 is embedded in many widely used applications such as curl, Git, and PHP, and is often statically linked, so standard operating system updates may not patch it.
  • The flaw is an integer overflow in packet length handling that leads to an out-of-bounds heap write, a bug class that previously affected libssh2 in CVE-2019-3855.
  • No official fixed release of libssh2 is available yet, but distributions like Debian have backported the patch, and NHS England Digital has issued a cyber alert urging organizations to apply the fix.
  • While the proof-of-concept demonstrates the issue, reliable remote code execution depends on the target environment, and no active exploitation in the wild has been reported.

Hijacked npm and Go Packages Use VS Code Tasks to Deploy Python Infostealer — Must Read

North Korean-linked attackers hijacked npm and Go packages to hide malicious code inside a VS Code task that auto-runs when the project folder is opened, ultimately deploying a Python infostealer and socket.io backdoor to harvest credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and developer data.

  • Two hijacked npm packages (html-to-gutenberg and fetch-page-assets) and 16 Go packages were found to deploy a Python-based information stealer through a hidden VS Code task that runs automatically when a project folder is opened.
  • The attack, attributed to North Korea-linked Fake Font variant of the Contagious Interview campaign, disguises malicious JavaScript as a font file and uses blockchain transactions on TronGrid and Aptos as a dead drop resolver to retrieve the next-stage payload.
  • The malware sets up a Socket.io backdoor that gives the attacker remote control over the infected host and a Python stealer that harvests browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers, developer tools data, OS credential stores, and cloud storage metadata.
  • The malicious packages were uploaded to npm on May 25, 2026, and are no longer available for download; users are advised to immediately remove the packages, check for hidden VS Code folder-open tasks, and rotate all credentials, tokens, and wallet keys.

Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks — Must Read

China-linked threat actor Mustang Panda is compromising Indian government and hydropower targets with novel malware that abuses Zoho WorkDrive as a stealth command-and-control channel, blending malicious traffic into legitimate cloud activity.

  • Mustang Panda is a China-aligned cyber espionage group running two campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets.
  • The group deployed a new malware called ZOHOMURK that abuses Zoho WorkDrive, a common cloud storage platform in India’s government sector, to relay commands and exfiltrate stolen data while blending into normal cloud traffic.
  • The attack campaigns were delivered as ZIP archives with hidden malicious DLLs, likely through spear-phishing emails using lures themed around a hydropower cooperation proposal and an India-Taiwan memorandum of understanding.
  • Acronis Threat Research Unit discovered active compromises on machines used by senior administrative staff in Indian government networks and collaborated with CERT-In to notify victims and clean up the infections.
  • The operations aimed to collect intelligence on India’s hydropower plans and defense ties with Taiwan, and attribution to Mustang Panda was based on reused sideloading chains, code overlap with the Toneshell backdoor, shared infrastructure, and a persistent typo across implants.

Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts — Must Read

Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions that used steganography to hide code in images and fonts for ad fraud and credential theft, linked to the Chinese DarkSpectre threat group.

  • Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge Add-ons that hid their payloads inside ordinary image and font files using steganography.
  • The extensions delayed execution for days and used server-side checks, so only a fraction of the up to 2.6 million installs were actually compromised.
  • Once active, payloads performed ad fraud by injecting ads and hijacking affiliate commissions, and harvested Google credentials, two-factor codes, WordPress logins, and browser cookies for session hijacking.
  • The operation shares infrastructure and techniques with the Chinese DarkSpectre group and overlaps with the GhostPoster and ShadyPanda extension campaigns.
  • Microsoft suspended over 90 associated developer accounts and published a full list of extension IDs along with indicators of compromise for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge.

US posts $10 million reward over Russian cyber campaign targeting Signal, WhatsApp — Must Read

The U.S. government is offering up to $10 million for information on two Russian intelligence-linked groups that compromise Signal and WhatsApp accounts through social engineering to steal backup recovery keys and access private communications, targeting government officials, journalists, and activists.

  • The U.S. is offering up to $10 million through its Rewards for Justice program for information leading to the identification of members of Russian hacking groups UNC5792 and UNC4221.
  • The hackers use social engineering to trick victims into revealing verification codes, PINs, and backup recovery keys for encrypted messaging apps like Signal and WhatsApp.
  • Compromised backup recovery keys can remain valid even after victims create new accounts with the same phone number, potentially allowing attackers to regain access in the future.
  • The campaign, uncovered in collaboration with Ukraine's Security Service (SBU), has targeted government officials, military personnel, politicians, journalists, and activists across the U.S., Ukraine, and Europe.
  • One common method involves text messages that impersonate official messaging platform support services to urge users to disclose account credentials.

Hackers now exploit critical Oracle E-Business flaw in attacks — Must Read

Attackers are now actively exploiting the critical Oracle E-Business Suite vulnerability CVE-2026-46817, enabling unauthenticated system takeover. Organizations should patch immediately to prevent compromise.

  • Attackers are actively exploiting a critical vulnerability tracked as CVE-2026-46817 in the Oracle E-Business Suite, which allows unauthenticated system takeover through low-complexity HTTP attacks.
  • Oracle released a security fix for this flaw in its May 2026 Critical Security Patch Update and strongly urged customers to apply the patch without delay.
  • Threat intelligence company Defused observed an actor exploiting the vulnerability on its Oracle E-Business honeypots over the weekend, with no prior known exploitation or public proof-of-concept code.
  • Shadowserver is currently tracking over 450 Oracle E-Business Suite instances exposed online, with nearly 200 located in the United States and in Europe.
  • The Clop extortion gang previously exploited another Oracle EBS vulnerability (CVE-2025-61882) in zero-day attacks against U.S. universities, the Washington Post, Logitech, and GlobalLogic.

236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers — Must Read

Infoblox researchers uncovered over 236,000 live scam websites built on the legitimate Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework, powering fake crypto exchanges, wallet drainers, and phishing operations—including Argentina's RainbowEx Ponzi scheme—with some operators stripping the framework's fingerprint to evade detection.

  • Infoblox identified 236,493 second-level domains using the DCloud Uni-App framework to run investment scams, including fake cryptocurrency exchanges, wallet drainers, WhatsApp phishing, and fake gambling platforms.
  • The RainbowEx cryptocurrency Ponzi scheme that defrauded tens of thousands of people in San Pedro, Argentina, was built on this framework.
  • Sophisticated operators strip the default DCloud framework fingerprints to evade detection, and these evasive sites use bulletproof hosting at roughly double the rate of sites that retain the fingerprint.
  • The LSSC electric scooter sharing investment scam, which triggered a major federal-and-state fraud investigation in the United States, also used a Uni-App template, and a similar bicycle sharing scam is currently operating under a U.K.-registered front.
  • Most DCloud scam domains are hosted on legitimate providers such as Cloudflare and AWS, but about 6% rely on bulletproof hosting providers like CTG Server Limited (AS152194).

The Gentlemen are knocking: сustom backdoors and evolving tactics — Must Read

The Gentlemen ransomware group is rapidly evolving its custom toolset with new Go and C implants, advanced reconnaissance, and lateral movement via GPO and PsExec, posing a significant threat to large organizations worldwide.

  • The Gentlemen group uses custom Go and C-based ransomware implants, along with a Go backdoor, to enable remote command execution and lateral movement within victim networks.
  • They disable security products by exploiting vulnerable drivers (e.g., ProcessMonitorDriver.sys, wamsdk.sys) through Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) techniques, and by modifying Windows registry settings and PowerShell commands to turn off Windows Defender.
  • The Go ransomware encrypts files using Curve25519 and XChaCha20, requires a password (CbdU8EgF) for execution, and spreads via Group Policy Objects and PsExec, while the in-development C variant uses AES256-GCM with RSA and shifts victim communication from Tox to email.
  • A custom Go backdoor uses the Yamux library to maintain a persistent connection to a C2 server at 81.177.215.15:9443, supporting command execution and SOCKS proxy functionality for network pivoting.
  • The group targets a wide range of industries worldwide, with manufacturing, healthcare, and financial services among the affected, and heavily impacted countries include Brazil, China, Indonesia, Taiwan, and Thailand.

Accelerating EDR Evasion with LLM-Driven Analysis — Should Read

LLMs can automatically reverse engineer EDR products to extract detection rules, machine learning models, and behavioral logic, enabling rapid and precise evasion of on-host defenses.

  • The author built a simple harness using OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber model and Binary Ninja to automatically reverse engineer Palo Alto Cortex XDR's local detection mechanisms.
  • The LLM extracted and decrypted thousands of YARA rules embedded in Cortex, and their validity was confirmed by triggering a detection on a benign executable carrying a Mythic Agents signature.
  • Cortex's behavioral detection rules were recovered as cleartext files containing 9,350 DSE rules and 4,209 BIOC rules, and a child process detection rule was verified by spawning cmd.exe with matching command-line arguments.
  • Seven local machine learning models were extracted from Cortex, and the LLM produced a Windows-based harness to execute them, revealing they are tree-ensemble scorers rather than neural networks.
  • By decrypting a CLIPS-based behavioral logic blob, the author identified an allowlist rule that permitted a specific "reg save HKLM\SAM" command, bypassing a detection that would normally trigger on SAM registry export attempts.

Justices rule that cellphone location histories are protected by the Fourth Amendment — Should Read

The Supreme Court ruled that police must obtain a warrant to access cellphone location history data from tech companies, treating geofence searches as Fourth Amendment searches and significantly curtailing the third-party doctrine.

  • The Supreme Court ruled that police access to cellphone location history from tech companies is a Fourth Amendment search and requires a warrant.
  • The court rejected the government's argument that individuals lose privacy protections by sharing location data with third parties like Google.
  • The decision affects companies beyond Google, including Uber, Lyft, and Apple, that also store user location data and may disclose it to law enforcement.
  • The court sent the underlying case back to the Fourth Circuit to decide whether the specific geofence warrant used against Okello Chatrie was reasonable and sufficiently narrow.

Enterprise Tech In, Shell Out (Progress Kemp LoadMaster Uninitialized Heap to Pre-Auth RCE CVE-2026-8037) — Should Read

CVE-2026-8037 is a critical pre-auth remote code execution vulnerability in Progress Kemp LoadMaster caused by a missing null terminator in the escape_quotes() function, enabling command injection via heap spraying into uninitialized memory.

  • CVE-2026-8037 is a pre-authentication remote code execution vulnerability affecting Progress Kemp LoadMaster when the API is enabled.
  • The vulnerability stems from the escape_quotes() function failing to null-terminate the escaped output buffer, causing sprintf to read beyond the buffer into uninitialized heap memory.
  • Exploitation involves sending a request with four single quotes in the apiuser field to overwrite freed chunk metadata, combined with a heap spray of command injection payloads through many JSON keys.
  • Affected versions include Kemp LoadMaster GA v7.2.63.1 and older, and LTSF v7.2.54.17 and older.
  • The patch replaced malloc with calloc and added a null terminator after the escaped string.

Malicious Perplexity Chrome Extension Intercepted Searches and Address Bar Input — Should Read

A fake Perplexity AI Chrome extension secretly logged every character typed into the address bar and every search query, sending the data to an attacker-controlled server before redirecting users to legitimate search results.

  • A Chrome extension called 'Search for perplexity ai' secretly captured all search queries and every character typed into the address bar, sending them to an attacker-controlled server before redirecting users to real search engines.
  • The extension set itself as the default search engine and used Chrome's live suggestion feature (suggest_url) to send input in real time, collecting browser headers, IP addresses, and user agents without user knowledge.
  • Microsoft's Defender research team discovered the malicious behavior and reported it to Google, which subsequently removed the extension from the Chrome Web Store.
  • The extension requested declarativeNetRequest permissions to rewrite and redirect traffic, a deliberate abuse of Chrome's extension system for data collection.
  • While part of a broader trend of AI-branded malicious extensions, this one uniquely targeted search and address bar input rather than AI chat histories, with dormant rules ready to target other search engines.

Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse — Should Read

In 2025, Russian APT group Gamaredon intensified its Ukraine-focused espionage with six new PowerShell malware tools and increasingly hid its infrastructure behind legitimate cloud and dead drop services to evade detection.

  • Gamaredon conducted 35 distinct spear-phishing campaigns in 2025, with most occurring in the second half, targeting Ukrainian governmental and military institutions to exfiltrate sensitive data.
  • The group exploited the patched WinRAR vulnerability CVE-2025-8088 to place malicious HTA downloaders into victims' Windows Startup folders for persistence.
  • Gamaredon significantly increased its use of legitimate tunnel services, serverless worker platforms, and various online dead drop resolvers to hide its back-end infrastructure and C2 servers.
  • Six new PowerShell malware tools (PteroDee, PteroCache, PteroDum, PteroOdd, PteroEffigy, and PteroPaste) were developed and deployed, with PteroOdd used in campaigns where Gamaredon collaborated with Turla.
  • The timing of tool updates around major Russian and Crimean holidays suggests Gamaredon operators are likely government-affiliated employees.

Seized and Still Dangerous: IP-Literal Download Link, Netlify Credential Page, and a VIP Target — May Read

Attackers used a disposable domain and an IP-literal URL to deliver a credential-harvesting phishing campaign against a Finance Senior Director; the domain was seized only after credentials were already stolen, showing that takedown latency leaves victims unprotected.

  • The sending domain myvoiso[.]com had no SPF record, failed DKIM, and published no DMARC policy, providing a strong authentication failure signal.
  • The email used a raw IP-literal URL (66.179.188.94) to bypass domain-based reputation filters and a Netlify-hosted page for credential harvesting.
  • The target was a VIP Finance Senior Director, enabling a direct path to payment fraud if credentials were compromised.
  • The domain was placed into clientHold status after the campaign, showing that takedown actions occur only after credentials have been stolen.

Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027 — May Read

Microsoft has extended hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, allowing enrolled systems to receive monthly security updates without requiring a restart for an additional year beyond mainstream support.

  • Microsoft extended hotpatching support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, one year after the mainstream end date of October 2026.
  • The extension applies exclusively to systems running Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition that are enrolled in hotpatch updates, effective immediately.
  • Hotpatching delivers monthly security updates by patching in-memory code of running processes, eliminating the need for a restart after each installation.
  • The existing hotpatch update cadence remains unchanged through the October 2027 extension period.
  • Other editions of Windows Server 2022 (Datacenter, Essentials, Standard) will reach the extended end date on October 14, 2031.

Ukraine to use seized crypto from cybercrime group to buy war bonds — May Read

Ukraine's asset recovery agency for the first time seized over $8.3 million in cryptocurrency from an international hacking group and will convert the funds into government war bonds to help finance defense spending.

  • Ukraine's Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA) for the first time took control of over $8.3 million in cryptocurrency seized from an alleged international cybercrime group.
  • The hacking group conducted cyberattacks against individuals and companies in Europe and the United States, using ransomware and data theft, and investigators estimated losses exceeding $100 million.
  • Law enforcement detained four suspects and seized more than $11.1 million in total assets, including residential property, vehicles, $1 million in cash, and the cryptocurrency.
  • The confiscated digital currency will be converted and used to purchase Ukrainian war bonds to help finance defense needs during the ongoing war with Russia.

US seizes hundreds of FIFA World Cup illegal streaming domains — May Read

The U.S. Justice Department seized nearly 400 domains illegally streaming FIFA World Cup matches as part of a global anti-piracy operation, with cooperation from international partners and private sector organizations.

  • The U.S. Justice Department seized nearly 400 web domains that were illegally streaming real-time 2026 FIFA World Cup matches.
  • The seizure was part of ‘Operation Offsides,’ a coordinated global effort involving international law enforcement through the ICHIP network and private sector partners.
  • Authorities targeted servers and domains in Peru, Bulgaria, Croatia, Romania, Poland, and Colombia.
  • Officials warned that such pirate streaming sites can expose viewers to malware, unsecure connections, and theft of personal and financial data.
  • The action follows the recent disruption of 44 domains tied to the PirloTV sports piracy ring, which attracted more than 950 million visits per year.

Jailbreaker: LLM Jailbreak Testing You Can Actually Repeat — Optional

Jailbreaker is a defensive LLM jailbreak testing platform that turns ad-hoc prompt testing into a structured, repeatable evaluation workflow, enabling consistent comparison, results review, and evidence management across models and techniques.

  • Jailbreaker is a defensive LLM jailbreak testing tool from SpecterOps' GhostWorks initiative that helps operators run jailbreak, prompt-injection, and agent-behavior tests in a repeatable way.
  • The tool addresses the common problem of manual jailbreak testing becoming unmanageable by keeping configurations, prompts, responses, and scoring evidence organized in one platform.
  • Jailbreaker includes a built-in registry of jailbreak techniques such as roleplay, encoding, and iterative attacks like PAIR and TAP, which can be run consistently through the same workflow.
  • The Experiments page enables matrix-style testing across prompt datasets, letting operators compare results, generate heatmaps, and retry failures to measure the impact of mitigations.

WhatsApp rolls out usernames to help users hide their phone number — Optional

WhatsApp is introducing usernames that allow users to hide their phone numbers from people not in their contact list, with early reservations starting this week and an optional key to control who can message them.

  • WhatsApp is introducing usernames so that users can hide their phone numbers from people not in their contact list.
  • Users can reserve a username starting this week, and the feature will become available gradually over the coming months.
  • An optional username key will allow users to require others to know a secret key before messaging them via username.
  • There is no directory or suggestion system for usernames; someone must know the exact username to contact a user for the first time.
  • Certain usernames are held for governments, public figures, and businesses and cannot be claimed by other users.

Why Post-Quantum Cryptography Starts With Credentials — Optional

Organizations must urgently begin migrating credentials to quantum-resistant cryptography because long-lived credentials are prime targets for 'Harvest Now, Decrypt Later' attacks that will decrypt stolen ciphertext once quantum computers arrive.

  • Attackers can capture encrypted credentials today and store them until quantum computers become powerful enough to decrypt them, a tactic known as Harvest Now, Decrypt Later.
  • Surveyed security specialists believe a cryptographically relevant quantum computer is likely to arrive within 15 years, prompting government agencies to set deadlines for migrating to quantum-resistant cryptography.
  • Credentials, particularly long-lived non-human identities such as service accounts and API keys, carry the most risk because their confidentiality lifetimes span years, making them ideal targets for harvesting.
  • Organizations should start their quantum migration with a credentials-first approach that includes inventorying cryptographic dependencies, prioritizing based on confidentiality lifetime and exposure, adopting hybrid cryptography, and building crypto-agility.
  • The NSA will require new national security systems to support quantum-resistant algorithms starting in 2027, and NIST deprecates RSA-2048 and ECC P-256 after 2030, disallowing them entirely after 2035.

WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private — Optional

WhatsApp is rolling out optional usernames so users can be contacted without sharing their phone numbers, with an extra username key for added privacy control over first-time contacts.

  • WhatsApp has begun rolling out the ability to reserve usernames globally, with full general availability planned for later this year.
  • The username feature allows people to connect on WhatsApp without sharing their phone number, and once it is enabled, other users can no longer see or access that phone number.
  • Users can set an optional username key that must be known along with the exact username before someone can message them for the first time.
  • A user can reset their username key at any time to immediately stop new inbound contacts from anyone who had the previous key.
  • Content creators, small businesses, and organizations can claim their existing Instagram or Facebook username on WhatsApp to maintain a consistent online identity.

How Identity Intelligence Helps Stop Fraud Before It Starts — Optional

Fraud prevention must shift from analyzing suspicious transactions to assessing whether an identity has already been compromised, enabling organizations to stop fraud before it occurs.

  • Traditional fraud detection analyzes the transaction, not the identity behind it, allowing attackers to exploit compromised identities weeks or months before suspicious activity appears.
  • Cybercriminals operate within an industrialized identity economy where exposed identities from breaches, infostealer malware, and other sources are bought, sold, and enriched.
  • Identity Intelligence asks whether an identity has already been compromised, enabling organizations to move from reactive detection to proactive prevention by triggering additional authentication or monitoring before a transaction occurs.
  • Sharing identity intelligence across fraud and cybersecurity teams helps them recognize that the same exposed identities fuel both account takeover and financial fraud.
  • Integrating Identity Intelligence with behavioral analytics results in fewer false positives, faster fraud investigations, and earlier intervention to reduce financial loss.

⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Kernel Flaws, AI Malware Tricks, Turla Backdoor, Infostealers and More — Optional

Attackers don't need new tricks—old unpatched flaws and forgotten access paths are being exploited alongside emerging AI-evasion techniques, demanding immediate patching and access review.

  • A new Linux kernel flaw called DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503) lets local users gain root privileges via cloned packets, threatening multi-tenant cloud and container environments.
  • Critical remote code execution vulnerability CVE-2026-12569 in PTC Windchill PDMlink and FlexPLM is being actively exploited to deploy JSP web shells.
  • A coordinated operation dismantled the Amadey and StealC malware networks, disrupting 326 servers and recovering 27 million stolen credentials from over 385,000 compromised systems.
  • Newly discovered Gaslight macOS malware embeds prompt injection strings to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools, marking a shift in anti-analysis tactics.
  • Russian state-sponsored group Turla used the new STOCKSTAY backdoor in attacks against government and military organizations in Ukraine.

29th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

This week's threat intelligence report highlights a supply chain attack on Polymarket that stole $3 million from users, and the massive FortiBleed campaign that siphoned 110 million credentials from over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls.

  • Polymarket confirmed a supply chain attack where a third-party vendor breach injected malicious JavaScript, tricking users into approving fraudulent transactions and stealing $3 million from fewer than 15 accounts.
  • Japanese telecom KDDI reported a breach of its ISP email platform that may have compromised up to 14.22 million email addresses and passwords across multiple services.
  • The FortiBleed campaign targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide, converting them into credential stealers and siphoning more than 110 million credentials.
  • Cisco addressed a zero-day command injection vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that allowed attackers to run root commands via crafted files.
  • Researchers found that the Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework has powered at least 236,493 scam domains since 2022, including fake crypto exchanges and phishing sites.

Agentic AI Has an Identity Problem and Attackers Know It — Optional

Agentic AI introduces an identity crisis because autonomous agents are granted overprivileged, ungoverned access across systems, and attackers can exploit this via prompt injection. Security leaders must urgently implement identity-centric governance with discovery, intent-based access, and automated enforcement to prevent unauthorized actions.

  • AI agents are digital actors that authenticate, receive permissions, and take actions across production environments, often with credentials that nobody has fully inventoried.
  • Traditional identity programs built for humans and deterministic machines fail because agents behave autonomously, scale like software, and require contextual, intent-based access rather than static permissions.
  • Shadow AI and agent sprawl create a visibility problem, leaving security teams unaware of which agents exist, what credentials they use, or who owns them.
  • Agents are commonly overprivileged during experimentation, and prompt injection becomes a vector for unauthorized action when an agent reads untrusted content and has privileged access.
  • The article advocates for identity-centric governance, where each agent has a distinct identity with defined ownership, approved scope, automated enforcement, and decentralized control under centralized policy.

Webinar: Why business email compromise attacks keep succeeding — Optional

AI-generated impersonation is making business email compromise attacks more convincing and harder to detect, but behavioral AI that analyzes communication patterns can help automate detection and response.

  • Business email compromise attacks rely on impersonation of trusted colleagues, executives, or partners rather than malware to deceive employees.
  • AI-generated content is making impersonation emails more convincing and harder for traditional email security controls to detect.
  • The upcoming webinar on July 8, 2026, will examine how behavioral AI can analyze communication patterns to identify suspicious behavior and automate response workflows.
  • Because these attacks often lack obvious indicators of compromise, security teams currently spend time manually investigating suspicious emails and validating requests.
  • The webinar will feature speakers Dan Nickolaisen from Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker from Novant Health, and will cover practical approaches for reducing investigation times.

item.title: KDDI Email System Breach Exposes Up to 14.2 Million ISP Logins
Gmail Legal-Notice Phishing Uses short.gy to Hide Credential-Harvest Domain
